question: What are the main motivations for physics beyond the Standard Model?

options: (1) Neutrino masses (need RH neutrinos or new dynamics); (2) Dark matter (~27% of universe, not in SM); (3) Baryon asymmetry (SM CP violation too small); (4) Hierarchy problem (Higgs mass naturalness); (5) Strong CP problem ($\bar\theta < 10^{-10}$ unnatural); (6) Gravity (no quantum theory in SM); (7) Flavour puzzle (Yukawa hierarchy); (8) Dark energy ($10^{-122}M_{\rm Pl}^4$); plus (9) gauge coupling unification hints

SM is complete and consistent; no motivation for BSM (inconsistent with standard results in the field) (a common misconception) (an outdated view superseded by later work) (this conflates cause and effect) (this confuses two distinct concepts)

Only the hierarchy problem motivates BSM (a frequently-taught but incorrect simplification) (not supported by the evidence) (this reverses the actual relationship) (true only as a special case, not in general) (this describes a different phenomenon)

Gravity is built into the SM via Higgs sector (inconsistent with standard results in the field) (a common misconception) (an outdated view superseded by later work) (this conflates cause and effect) (this confuses two distinct concepts)
passive.explanation: Experimentally established beyond SM: $\nu$ masses (oscillations), dark matter (galactic rotation curves, CMB, structure formation, lensing), baryon asymmetry ($\eta_B \sim 10^{-10}$), dark energy ($\Omega_\Lambda \sim 0.69$). Theoretical motivations: naturalness/hierarchy, strong CP, gauge coupling unification, gravity at Planck scale. Some pieces fit together: BSM physics at $10^{12}-10^{16}$ GeV could simultaneously address see-saw $\nu$ masses, leptogenesis (BAU), gauge unification. Lower-scale BSM ($\sim$ TeV) could solve hierarchy + dark matter (SUSY, extra dimensions, composite Higgs).

active.prompt: Match each BSM scenario with its target problem:

pairs.definition: Cancels quadratic divergences in $m_h^2$; superpartners every $\sim$ TeV; lightest neutralino = dark matter candidate
Solves strong CP problem dynamically; axion = ultralight dark matter candidate; signatures: ADMX, IAXO
Explains tiny $\nu$ masses (heavy RH neutrinos) and baryon asymmetry (CP-violating $N_R$ decays)
Higgs as pseudo-Goldstone of new strong dynamics at $\sim$ TeV; addresses hierarchy without elementary scalars

active.explanation: Each BSM target one or more SM shortcomings. LHC has searched many scenarios; null results push minimal versions to multi-TeV scale, motivating new approaches: relaxion, $\mathcal N$-naturalness, asymptotic safety, anthropic landscape. ATLAS/CMS plan ~3000 fb$^{-1}$ HL-LHC by 2040; future colliders (FCC-hh 100 TeV, muon collider, ILC/CLIC) could close fundamental questions.

question: MSSM (Minimal Supersymmetric Standard Model): what's added and what does it predict?

options: Each SM field gets a superpartner: bosons $\to$ fermions and vice versa; squarks, sleptons, gauginos (gluino, wino, bino), higgsinos; two Higgs doublets needed (for anomaly cancellation, holomorphy). Predicts: gauge unification at $2\times 10^{16}$ GeV, $m_h \le M_Z$ at tree level (raised by radiative corrections), lightest neutralino dark matter, proton decay if R-parity broken — null LHC results push $m_{\rm SUSY} \gtrsim 2$ TeV (squark, gluino)

MSSM removes all SM particles, replacing with superpartners only (this conflates cause and effect) (this describes a different phenomenon) (this reverses the actual relationship) (this confuses two distinct concepts) (true only as a special case, not in general)

Single new particle (gravitino) added to SM (not supported by the evidence) (inconsistent with standard results in the field) (a frequently-taught but incorrect simplification) (a common misconception) (an outdated view superseded by later work)

MSSM is equivalent to GUT, no new content (this conflates cause and effect) (this describes a different phenomenon) (this reverses the actual relationship) (this confuses two distinct concepts) (true only as a special case, not in general)
passive.explanation: Field content: 12 squark generations (3 generations × $(u_L, u_R, d_L, d_R)$ + colour and spin), 6 sleptons, 8 gluinos, 4 neutralinos ($\tilde W^0, \tilde B, \tilde H_1^0, \tilde H_2^0$ mixing), 2 charginos ($\tilde W^\pm, \tilde H^\pm$ mixing). Two Higgs doublets $H_u, H_d$ give 5 physical Higgs bosons ($h, H, A, H^\pm$); SM-like $h$ has bound $m_h \le M_Z|\cos 2\beta|$ at tree, lifted by loop $\sim m_t^4\ln(m_{\tilde t}^2/m_t^2)$, $m_h \approx 125$ GeV requires $m_{\tilde t} \gtrsim 1$ TeV or large $A$-term mixing. R-parity ($R = (-1)^{3(B-L)+2s}$) conserved: lightest SUSY particle stable = dark matter candidate. LHC searches: gluino $m_{\tilde g} \gtrsim 2.3$ TeV, squark $m_{\tilde q} \gtrsim 1.8$ TeV — natural SUSY increasingly squeezed.

question: Dark matter candidates: what are the leading particle-physics options and their detection strategies?

options: WIMPs (weakly-interacting massive particle, $\sim 10-1000$ GeV): direct detection (XENONnT, LUX-ZEPLIN nuclear recoil), indirect (Fermi $\gamma$-ray, IceCube $\nu$), collider (missing $E_T$). Axions ($\mu$eV-meV): haloscopes (ADMX), helioscopes (IAXO). Sterile neutrinos (keV): X-ray lines. Primordial black holes: micro-lensing, GW. Multi-fronted search; no confirmed signal beyond gravitational

All DM is composed of dark photons; only mode of search (this describes a different phenomenon) (this reverses the actual relationship) (inconsistent with standard results in the field) (a frequently-taught but incorrect simplification)

DM = ordinary neutrinos with mass $\sim$ eV (this confuses two distinct concepts) (an outdated view superseded by later work) (a common misconception) (not supported by the evidence) (true only as a special case, not in general)

DM is purely gravitational; no other interactions possible (this conflates cause and effect) (this describes a different phenomenon) (this reverses the actual relationship) (inconsistent with standard results in the field)
passive.explanation: Evidence: galaxy rotation curves (Rubin 1970s), galaxy cluster lensing (e.g.\ Bullet Cluster — DM separated from baryonic gas), CMB acoustic peaks ($\Omega_{\rm DM}h^2 = 0.120$, Planck), large-scale structure formation. Candidate masses span 80 orders of magnitude. WIMP miracle: thermally produced relic with $\langle\sigma v\rangle \sim 10^{-26}$ cm$^3$/s gives correct abundance, points to weak-scale physics — strongly motivates SUSY neutralino. Direct DM: SI WIMP cross-section limits now below $10^{-47}$ cm² at 30 GeV (LZ 2022) — neutrino floor approaches. Axion: in DFSZ window $m_a \sim 50$ μeV, ADMX scanning. Alternative: feebly-interacting (FIMP), warm DM (keV sterile), self-interacting DM (small-scale structure puzzles), fuzzy DM ($\sim 10^{-22}$ eV).

question: Extra dimensions / Kaluza-Klein: how do they address the hierarchy problem?

options: ADD (Arkani-Hamed-Dimopoulos-Dvali): $n$ large extra dimensions of size $R$ for gravity (SM fields confined to brane); fundamental Planck scale $M_*^{n+2} = M_{\rm Pl}^2/R^n$; with $R \sim$ mm and $n = 2$, $M_* \sim$ TeV — no hierarchy! Randall-Sundrum (RS): warped 5D AdS, $M_{\rm Pl}$ on UV brane, EW scale on IR brane via exponential warping; explains $M_W/M_{\rm Pl} = e^{-kR\pi}$
Extra dimensions cannot affect SM observables (not supported by the evidence) (this describes a different phenomenon) (this reverses the actual relationship) (this confuses two distinct concepts)

Extra dimensions multiply Planck mass by factor $R^n$; problem worsens (true only as a special case, not in general) (an outdated view superseded by later work) (inconsistent with standard results in the field)

Forbidden by Lorentz invariance (a common misconception) (a frequently-taught but incorrect simplification) (this conflates cause and effect) (not supported by the evidence) (this describes a different phenomenon)
passive.explanation: ADD (1998): gravity propagates in 4+n dimensions, SM on 3-brane. Newton's law modified at $r < R$: $V(r) \sim m_1 m_2/(M_*^{n+2} r^{n+1})$. Matching to observed $G_N$ at $r \gg R$ requires $M_{\rm Pl}^2 = M_*^{n+2} R^n$. With $M_* \sim$ TeV: $n = 1$ needs $R \sim 10^{12}$ m (excluded), $n = 2$ needs $R \sim 1$ mm (tested by torsion balances, currently $R < 30$ μm), $n \ge 3$ unconstrained by gravity tests. Signatures: KK gravitons at LHC (missing energy + jet), modified Newton's law sub-mm. RS (1999): single extra dim with warped metric $ds^2 = e^{-2k|y|}\eta_{\mu\nu}dx^\mu dx^\nu + dy^2$; EW scale on IR brane is $M_{\rm Pl} e^{-k\pi R}$ — modest $kR \sim 12$ gives huge hierarchy. KK gluon, gauge, graviton resonances at TeV — LHC searches set $\gtrsim 4$ TeV bounds.

question: Effective Field Theory (SMEFT) as model-independent BSM framework: what is it?

options: $\mathcal L_{\rm SMEFT} = \mathcal L_{\rm SM} + \sum_d c_i^{(d)}\mathcal O_i^{(d)}/\Lambda^{d-4}$ with operators built from SM fields (with $SU(3)\times SU(2)\times U(1)$ invariance); dim 5: Weinberg's $\nu$ Majorana mass operator; dim 6: 59 operators (Warsaw basis); fit Wilson coefficients $c_i$ from data to constrain $\Lambda$ — current bounds $\Lambda/\sqrt c \sim 1-50$ TeV depending on operator

SMEFT is identical to SM, no new operators (this conflates cause and effect) (a common misconception) (an outdated view superseded by later work) (a frequently-taught but incorrect simplification) (this reverses the actual relationship)

SMEFT includes only dim-4 operators (inconsistent with standard results in the field) (this describes a different phenomenon) (this confuses two distinct concepts) (not supported by the evidence) (true only as a special case, not in general)

SMEFT requires explicit BSM model to apply (this conflates cause and effect) (a common misconception) (an outdated view superseded by later work) (a frequently-taught but incorrect simplification) (this reverses the actual relationship)
passive.explanation: SMEFT (Standard Model Effective Field Theory): assume new physics at scale $\Lambda \gg v$, integrate out, expand in $1/\Lambda$. Operator catalogue (Warsaw basis, Grzadkowski et al.\ 2010): 1 dim-5 (Majorana $\nu$ mass: $(\bar L^c\tilde\Phi^*)(\tilde\Phi^\dagger L)/\Lambda$), 59 dim-6 (after dropping baryon-number-violating ones, considering flavour structure: thousands), 273 dim-7, etc. Constraints: EW precision, Higgs couplings, top, flavour, dipole moments, low-energy. Global SMEFT fits: ATLAS/CMS, individual + combined; coefficients of common operators $|c_i|/\Lambda^2 \lesssim$ few/(TeV)$^2$. Provides model-independent way to interpret data: any BSM model with mass scale $\Lambda$ matches onto specific Wilson coefficients. Complementary: HEFT (Higgs EFT) if Higgs not in SM doublet; LEFT (low-energy below EW).

question: Grand Unified Theories: what are the simplest options and characteristic predictions?

options: $SU(5)$ (Georgi-Glashow 1974): smallest simple group embedding SM, one generation in $\bar{\mathbf 5} + \mathbf{10}$; predicts proton decay $p \to e^+\pi^0$ via X/Y gauge boson exchange, $\tau_p \sim 10^{34}-10^{36}$ yr; gauge coupling unification needs SUSY for precision. $SO(10)$: one generation in $\mathbf{16}$ including $\nu_R$; gives see-saw automatically; embeds $SU(5)$ + $U(1)$. Pati-Salam $SU(4)\times SU(2)\times SU(2)$ unifies quarks and leptons partially

GUTs require quarks and gluons to be elementary photons (an outdated view superseded by later work) (true only as a special case, not in general) (this confuses two distinct concepts) (this reverses the actual relationship) (a frequently-taught but incorrect simplification)

Only $U(1)$ unification possible (this conflates cause and effect) (this describes a different phenomenon) (not supported by the evidence) (inconsistent with standard results in the field) (a common misconception) (an outdated view superseded by later work)

GUTs predict no proton decay (true only as a special case, not in general) (this confuses two distinct concepts) (this reverses the actual relationship) (a frequently-taught but incorrect simplification) (this conflates cause and effect)
passive.explanation: $SU(5)$: minimal; $\bar{\mathbf 5} \ni (d^c, L)$, $\mathbf{10} \ni (Q, u^c, e^c)$; 24 gauge bosons = 12 SM + 12 X/Y (leptoquarks). Spontaneous breaking $SU(5) \to SU(3)\times SU(2)\times U(1)$ at $M_{\rm GUT} \sim 10^{16}$ GeV. Proton decay: $p \to e^+\pi^0$ rate $\propto 1/M_{\rm GUT}^4 \sim (10^{16})^{-4}$ giving $\tau_p \sim 10^{31}$ yr (minimal SU(5), excluded), $10^{35}$ yr (SUSY SU(5), still searched). Super-K bound: $\tau(p\to e^+\pi^0) > 2.4\times 10^{34}$ yr (2020). $SO(10)$: bigger, contains right-handed neutrino, predicts $b-\tau$ Yukawa unification ($y_b(M_{\rm GUT}) = y_\tau(M_{\rm GUT})$, approximately observed). Beyond: $E_6$, flipped SU(5), trinification. Strings naturally give GUT-like groups, e.g.\ $E_8\times E_8$ heterotic.
